one_line_takeaway: BlindU lets an FL server unlearn a user's data using only an information-bottleneck-compressed, DP-masked representation the user computes locally, raising CIFAR10 reconstruction-attack MSE from 57.76 to 421.9 versus prior gradient-based federated unlearning while matching or beating it on backdoor-removal utility, at the cost of a beta/SR-controlled privacy-utility trade-off that becomes more pronounced when unlearning normal (non-backdoored) samples.
key_finding: On the backdoor-unlearning benchmark at EDR=6% (Table III), BlindU (both BlindUw and BlindUw/o variants) matches or exceeds the HBFU and VBU baselines on utility while achieving comparable or better erasure of the backdoor, without ever exposing raw data to the server: e.g., on CIFAR10, BlindUw reaches 75.83% test accuracy with 7.11% accuracy on the erased backdoored set (versus HBFU 75.83%/8.11% and VBU 65.45%/7.92%); on the Adult dataset, HBFU fails to unlearn the backdoor at all (99.99% accuracy still retained on De after 'unlearning'), whereas BlindUw drives that down to 9.44%. For privacy protection, the paper's headline number is that BlindU increases the privacy-reconstruction MSE on CIFAR10 from 57.76 (existing best method) to 421.9, and reconstruction MSE / membership-inference AUC both improve (attacker performance drops) as the IB compression ratio beta increases and as the masking sampling ratio SR decreases, consistent with the paper's theoretical privacy bound linking (beta, SR) to a mutual-information/DP privacy descriptor. At SR=60% the derived DP guarantee is (0.597, 0.452)-DP. On the harder task of unlearning normal (non-backdoored) IID samples, the paper's own results show a larger utility cost, e.g., on the Adult dataset BlindUw only reduces accuracy on De to 44.33% (vs. HBFU's 80.89%, worse forgetting) while VBU achieves the lowest 7.82% but at a much lower overall test accuracy (51.32%), indicating BlindU does not dominate on every metric in this harder setting.
core_contribution: The paper formalizes 'erasing-data privacy-preserving machine unlearning': the problem of letting a federated-learning (FL) server unlearn a user's specified samples without the user ever disclosing the raw erasing data De=(Xe,Ye) to the server. It proposes BlindU, which assumes the FL model was trained with an information-bottleneck (IB) objective (a compressor plus an approximator). The unlearning user locally downloads only the trained compressor, applies a novel noise-free differential-privacy (DP) masking step (two sampling strategies, with-replacement 'BlindUw' and without-replacement 'BlindUw/o', governed by a sampling ratio SR) to the raw erasing features, then compresses the masked input into a representation Ze and uploads only Ce=(Ze,Ye) to the server. The server then jointly unlearns the compressor and approximator using two purpose-built unlearning modules operating solely on Ze/Ye (never on Xe): (1) an approximator-unlearning term that minimizes mutual information between Ze and Ye, and (2) a compressor-unlearning term that, since the server lacks access to Xe, instead minimizes mutual information between Ze and auxiliary representations the compressor produces on unrelated/random auxiliary inputs. These two forgetting objectives are combined with a utility-retention objective via a Multiple Gradient Descent Algorithm (MGDA) that adaptively balances the trade-off. The paper also gives a formal privacy analysis relating the compression ratio beta and masking sampling ratio SR to an information-theoretic privacy bound and a derived (epsilon,delta)-DP guarantee for the masking step (concretely SR=60% is stated to give (0.597, 0.452)-DP).
threat_model: Actor: the FL server (or any party that receives the unlearning request) is treated as an untrusted/curious observer that must not be able to reconstruct or infer membership of the erasing data. Constraint carried over from the FL setting: the server is 'strictly limited to accessing individual data' and cannot see raw Xe at any point, including during the unlearning step itself (this is the paper's stated departure from prior federated-unlearning work, which the authors say leaks privacy because gradients computed from a very small unlearning batch, sometimes a single sample, are more vulnerable to reconstruction than gradients from a full local batch). Attacks the paper defends against and empirically evaluates: (1) privacy/data reconstruction attacks that try to recover the original erasing images from whatever the server observes (raw gradients for baselines, or the masked-compressed representation Ze for BlindU), measured by reconstruction MSE; (2) membership inference attacks (MIA) that try to determine whether a target sample was in the training set of the original model but removed from the unlearned model, measured by attack AUC. No adversarial-training-time attacker (e.g., poisoning, backdoor injection by a malicious client) is in scope for defense; backdoors are used only as an evaluation proxy for whether unlearning actually removed a sample's influence.
limitations: (1) BlindU requires the original FL model to have been trained with an information-bottleneck objective; it is not a retrofit for arbitrary already-trained models, and the compression ratio beta is fixed at original-training time so the unlearning user cannot adjust it post hoc (only the masking sampling ratio SR is user-selectable). (2) The privacy-utility trade-off is explicit and directional: increasing beta or decreasing SR improves the paper's own privacy metrics but 'degrades the maximal achievable utility to some extent' (Section VI), and the experiments confirm real utility costs, especially in the normal-IID-sample unlearning setting versus the backdoor setting. (3) Baselines (HBFU, BFU) are evaluated under an 'ideal FL scenario' the authors say favors those methods (all users cooperate in retraining), which the authors themselves flag, and other federated-unlearning cluster-selection methods [11,12] and exact/retraining methods requiring full-dataset access were excluded from comparison as inapplicable to the privacy-preserving setting rather than being benchmarked. (4) Evaluated only on standard image datasets (MNIST/CIFAR10/CIFAR100/TinyImageNet) and one tabular dataset (Adult); no evaluation on text, graph, or other non-vision modalities. (5) The unlearning-effectiveness metric relies on the indirect backdoor-removal proxy plus test/erasing-set accuracy rather than a formal unlearning guarantee (e.g., certified removal), and the formal privacy analysis (Section VI) is a partial theoretical characterization that the authors explicitly say they only 'complement' empirically rather than prove tightly.

quantitative_results: CIFAR10 privacy-reconstruction MSE increased from 57.76 (existing best method) to 421.9 with BlindU
SR=60% DP masking yields a derived (0.597, 0.452)-DP guarantee
Backdoor unlearning at EDR=6%, MNIST: HBFU 95.86%/5.05% (test/De acc), VBU 88.32%/2.02%, BlindUw 94.55%/1.89%, BlindUw/o 94.65%/1.56%
Backdoor unlearning at EDR=6%, CIFAR10: HBFU 75.83%/8.11%, VBU 65.45%/7.92%, BlindUw 75.83%/7.11%, BlindUw/o 74.39%/6.57%
Backdoor unlearning at EDR=6%, Adult: HBFU 85.45%/99.99% (fails to unlearn), VBU 64.64%/8.45%, BlindUw 84.34%/9.44%, BlindUw/o 84.05%/7.25%
Normal-sample unlearning at EDR=6%, Adult: HBFU 83.03%/80.89%, VBU 51.32%/7.82%, BlindUw 84.08%/44.33%, BlindUw/o 84.05%/34.02%

Increasing IB compression ratio beta and decreasing masking sampling ratio SR both increase reconstruction MSE (better privacy) and decrease membership-inference AUC (better privacy), consistent across MNIST and CIFAR10 sweeps
baselines_compared: VBU (variational Bayesian unlearning, no privacy protection for unlearning data)
